text: The video demonstrates a thoracoscopic esophagectomy. The surgeon identifies anatomical landmarks including the vertebra, esophagus, azygos arch, trachea, subclavian vessel, intercostal spaces, rib indentations, and diaphragm. A small incision is made on the dorsal peritoneum using a monopolar hook, allowing carbon dioxide to enter and expand the surgical plane. The thoracic duct area is identified, and small vessels from the aorta are visualized and managed. A larger vessel is sealed using a Ligasure device. The dissection continues with careful traction to mobilize the peritoneum towards the hiatus, avoiding carbon dioxide escape into the abdominal cavity. The patient is in a prone position with ports in the fifth, sixth, and seventh intercostal spaces.

The surgeon uses three 5mm ports, including a 5mm telescope, to perform thoracoscopic dissection. The inferior pulmonary ligament is taken down using cautery, with care taken to avoid the lung parenchyma visible in the field. Traction is applied to open avascular planes for esophageal mobilization. The pulmonary vein is identified by its bluish hue and pulsations. An incision is made on the ventral peritoneum, revealing the vagus nerve and trachea, which appears white and collapsed. Dissection continues around the arch of the azygos to release peri-esophageal tissues, aiming for complete esophageal mobilization.

The surgeon continues the dissection beyond the esophagus, revealing the pericardium with visible heart pulsations. Loose areolar tissues are sharply dissected using a monopolar hook, opening up the planes and encircling the esophagus. The pleura of the left lung is visible as a thin membrane, and care is taken to avoid opening it. The vagus nerve is identified on the left side, and dissection proceeds in avascular planes with short bursts of cautery. The anterior dissection is performed between the aorta and the left bronchus, with small branches from the aorta being carefully managed.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the esophagus in a narrow space, avoiding injury to vascular structures such as the left bronchus. Using a monopolar hook, fibrous attachments are released with short bursts of cautery, aided by carbon dioxide insufflation. The pericardium is exposed, and lymphovascular tissues surrounding the esophagus are dissected and removed en bloc. The pleura is identified and preserved to prevent injury. The surgeon meticulously dissects fatty tissue from the pericardium, ensuring hemostatic control. Infracranial lymph nodes, appearing black due to smoking history, are handled carefully to avoid bleeding. The dissection continues on the ventral aspect to visualize the pulmonary vein.

The surgeon uses a monopolar hook to release fibrous attachments around the esophagus, working meticulously near the pulmonary vein to avoid bleeding. The pericardium is visible on the right side, and small fibrous tissues are dissected sharply. The esophagus is retracted dorsally to facilitate dissection on the ventral side, revealing flimsy tissues that are easily removed. The dissection progresses towards the cranial end, focusing on the area between the bronchus and pericardium, with the membranous portion of the trachea visible. Lymph nodes adjacent to the left bronchus are dissected, and lymphovascular structures are removed, contributing to the specimen.

The surgeon carefully dissects lymph node tissue and surrounding fatty tissue near the bifurcation of the bronchus, using traction and counter traction to safely separate fibrotic attachments. The bronchus is clearly visible, and the dissection proceeds with caution to avoid thermal injury to the membranous portion of the trachea. The subcarinal group of lymph nodes is gradually removed, ensuring hemostasis by coagulating small vessels. The esophagus is mobilized, and periesophageal tissues are dissected from the anterior aspect between the esophagus and pericardium, revealing the vagus nerve without disturbing it above the bifurcation level.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the bifurcation of the bronchus and trachea, using short bursts of cautery to minimize thermal spread. The membranous portion of the bronchus is visible, and the dissection includes lymph nodes near the bifurcation. A vessel sealing device is used to safely coagulate and divide small vessels, ensuring no injury to the bronchus. The esophagus is mobilized, and the infracranial portion is cleared of lymphovascular structures. The pericardium and left bronchus are clearly visible, with no major bleeding observed.

The surgeon observes the bulging and pulsation of the subclavian vessels and the superior vena cava lateral to them. The trachea is visible, and lymphovascular tissues and peritoneum are being incised to complete the dissection up to the thoracic inlet. The lung is collapsed due to pneumothorax, and small vessels and fibrotic strands between the trachea and esophagus are being carefully dissected. The right bronchial artery is identified and preserved during the dissection, ensuring no inadvertent injury to surrounding structures.
